coredata.dc:title: Photoluminescent surface-functionalized graphene quantum dots for spontaneous interfacial homeotropic orientation of liquid crystals
coredata.dc:creator: Son, Intae
Son, Seung-Rak
An, Jongil
Choi, Jin-Wook
Kim, Soyern
Lee, Woo Young
Lee, Jun Hyup
coredata.dc:description: An interfacial molecular array of octadecylamine-functionalized graphene quantum dots (GQDs) was fabricated to simultaneously realize photoluminescence characteristics and molecular orientation for liquid crystals (LCs). The functionalized GQDs were synthesized using a simple bottom-up method by first grafting them with octadecylamine. The subsequent interfacial self-assembly of these functionalized GQDs in an LC medium formed a nanostructured molecular array on an indium tin oxide (ITO) electrode. Experiments revealed that the hydrophobic GQDs spontaneously covered the hydrophilic ITO surface through polar intermolecular interactions, and the resulting GQD layer induced the uniform vertical orientation of the LC molecules at the ITO interface owing to van der Waals interactions of long side chains. In addition, the unique photoluminescence of pure GQDs in the blue region was maintained even in an LC mixture with a low concentration of functionalized GQDs. A new type of homeotropically oriented LC device based on the nanostructured layer of functionalized GQDs exhibited superior electro-optical properties and a 375% improvement in response time for turn-off switching of the device relative to those of a conventional polymer layer. This functionalized GQD-based device simultaneously achieved the spontaneous alignment of the LCs, blue photoluminescence emission, and high-speed response performance for turn-off switching of the device. In addition, our method is cost-effective and encompasses a simple fabrication process for developing functional controllable surfaces without requiring a tedious multi-step manufacturing process.
coredata.dcterms:subject: Graphene quantum dot
Homeotropic orientation
Liquid crystal
Nanostructured layer
Self-assembly

xocs:item-toc-entry.xocs:item-toc-section-title: Preparation of homeotropically oriented LC cell with O-GQDs

xocs:funding.xocs:funding-agency: Ministry of Science and ICT, South Korea

xocs:funding-list.xocs:funding-text: This work was supported by a National Research Foundation of Korea (NRF) grant funded by the Korea Government (MSIT) (No. NRF-2018R1A5A1024127 ).

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: (a) Synthesis of the surface-functionalized GQDs. (b) Schematic illustration of construction of the nanostructured O-GQD layer for LC orientation.

(a) FT-IR spectra of octadecylamine, glycine, citric acid, and O-GQDs. (b) Photographic images of a 0.1 wt% O-GQD solution in hexane under daylight and UV light. (c) PL spectra of the O-GQD solutions at different excitation wavelengths of 300–380 nm.

(a) Water contact angles of bare ITO and nanostructured O-GQD layers prepared using different concentrations. (b) 3D image of the self-constructed O-GQD layer prepared with an O-GQD concentration of 0.1 wt%.
(a) Photographic images of the LC mixtures with different O-GQD concentrations under daylight and UV light. (b) PL spectra of the same mixtures and pure LC at an excitation wavelength of 380 nm.

POM images of the LC devices prepared using (a) the conventional PI layer and the nanostructured O-GQD layers with an O-GQD concentration of (b) 0.05 wt%, (c) 0.1 wt%, (d) 0.5 wt%, and (e) 1.0 wt%. (f) V–T curves and (g) response time curves of the LC devices prepared using the above-described layers.

head.ce:title: Photoluminescent surface-functionalized graphene quantum dots for spontaneous interfacial homeotropic orientation of liquid crystals

ce:affiliation.ce:label: a
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: Center for Coating Materials and Processing, Seoul National University, Seoul 08826, Republic of Korea

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: Center for Coating Materials and Processing

ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: Center for Coating Materials and Processing, Seoul National University, Seoul 08826, Republic of Korea.

ce:affiliation.ce:label: b
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: Department of Chemical Engineering, Soongsil University, Seoul 06978, Republic of Korea
ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: Department of Chemical Engineering, Soongsil University, Seoul 06978, Republic of Korea

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: An interfacial molecular array of octadecylamine-functionalized graphene quantum dots (GQDs) was fabricated to simultaneously realize photoluminescence characteristics and molecular orientation for liquid crystals (LCs). The functionalized GQDs were synthesized using a simple bottom-up method by first grafting them with octadecylamine. The subsequent interfacial self-assembly of these functionalized GQDs in an LC medium formed a nanostructured molecular array on an indium tin oxide (ITO) electrode. Experiments revealed that the hydrophobic GQDs spontaneously covered the hydrophilic ITO surface through polar intermolecular interactions, and the resulting GQD layer induced the uniform vertical orientation of the LC molecules at the ITO interface owing to van der Waals interactions of long side chains. In addition, the unique photoluminescence of pure GQDs in the blue region was maintained even in an LC mixture with a low concentration of functionalized GQDs. A new type of homeotropically oriented LC device based on the nanostructured layer of functionalized GQDs exhibited superior electro-optical properties and a 375% improvement in response time for turn-off switching of the device relative to those of a conventional polymer layer. This functionalized GQD-based device simultaneously achieved the spontaneous alignment of the LCs, blue photoluminescence emission, and high-speed response performance for turn-off switching of the device. In addition, our method is cost-effective and encompasses a simple fabrication process for developing functional controllable surfaces without requiring a tedious multi-step manufacturing process.

ce:section.ce:para: Quantum dots (QDs) are attracting considerable attention as next-generation semiconductor materials because of their narrow emission spectra, tunable colors, and high stability [1–6]. Owing to these attractive properties, they have been utilized and studied in various devices such as displays, batteries, solar cells, and biosensors [7–11]. In particular, quantum dot light-emitting diodes that utilize the electroluminescence (EL) characteristics of QDs are used not only in display devices [12,13] but also as color conversion films for liquid crystal displays that exploit their photoluminescence (PL) properties [14,15]. Despite these advantages, the commercial utilization of QD materials has been limited because of their toxicity, which is caused by the presence of heavy metals (e.g., Cd and Pb) [16,17]. To overcome this toxicity issue, heavy-metal-free QDs based on indium phosphide (InP) have been investigated; however, devices based on such QDs have been less efficient than those based on heavy metal QDs, thereby inhibiting their commercial application [18]. In addition to InP-based QD materials, graphene quantum dots (GQDs), a zero-dimensional derivative of graphene, have low toxicity as well as large specific surface areas and broad luminescence spectra [19–22]. Because of these intrinsic properties, GQDs are attracting attention for applications in biomedical fields such as bio-imaging, cancer diagnosis, and drug delivery as well as for the development of optoelectronic devices [23–28]. However, the surface of GQDs requires modification owing to strong π–π bonding, wherein the carboxylic acid portion is most commonly modified. In the biomedical field, the solubility of GQDs in water has been reportedly improved by functionalizing their surface with sulfonic acid or with glucose to enhance the biocompatibility of GQDs [29,30]. In addition, GQDs that show color emission have been prepared by doping them with S, N, and B [31–34]. Other applications for doped GQDs include their use as electrocatalysts for the oxygen reduction reaction [35], detectors for the bio-imaging of living cells [36], and functional materials to improve the electrical performance of micro-supercapacitors [37]. As such, the electrical properties of GQDs have received particular attention [38–40]. However, to the best of our knowledge, no studies have employed them to control molecular orientation based on specific intermolecular interactions.

With the development of different types of flexible displays, such as curved, foldable, and stretchable, studies on next-generation displays are growing in importance. Currently, most flexible displays adopt organic light-emitting diodes; however, several studies have developed flexible LC displays based on new alignment materials [41–43]. One of the most important factors in implementing LC molecules in a display is controlling their initial arrangement, which can be classified into twisted nematic, in-plane switching, and vertical alignment modes. Industrially, polyimides (PIs) with long alkyl side chains are commonly employed to control this initial configuration. However, the formation of a PI layer requires a long curing time and high process temperatures over multiple steps, which are fatal for the plastic substrates that are applied in flexible displays. To overcome this limitation, a few studies have avoided the use of PI altogether [41–43].

Thus, we herein propose an elegant method that can efficiently control the initial configuration of LC molecules and simultaneously provide strong PL emission based on the facile self-assembly of octadecylamine-functionalized GQDs inside the LCs. For simplicity, these functionalized GQDs are hereafter referred to as O-GQDs. Owing to the presence of hydrophilic functional groups (-OH, -COOH) in the O-GQDs, they can spontaneously self-assemble on the hydrophilic surface of an indium tin oxide (ITO) substrate in the confined LC cell through polar intermolecular interactions [44,45]. In addition, the use of octadecylamine groups during O-GQD preparation results in the presence of a hydrophobic long alkyl chain structure, which is expected to lead to a stable, uniform homeotropic orientation of LC molecules [46,47]. Furthermore, the novel nanostructured O-GQD layer can be constructed over a large area at a low cost through a simple doping method without an additional coating or a high-temperature process. Moreover, this layer can successfully afford the adaptive control of LC alignment and remarkable electro-optical properties comparable to those of commercial alignment materials. Our proposed method is expected to be applicable in the preparation of functional flexible displays and self-emitting optoelectronic devices.

Citric acid monohydrate (99.5%) was obtained from Daesung (Siheung, Korea). Glycine (99.0%), octadecylamine (85.0%), and ethanol (99.0%) were purchased from TCI Chemicals (Tokyo, Japan). Negative nematic LCs (T NI = 75 °C, ∆n = 0.095, ∆ε = −3.1) were used for the LC device. A UV-curable sealant (SD-2×) and 3.25 μm spherical spacer (SP-203XX) were obtained from Sekisui Chemical (Tokyo, Japan). Commercial PI (AL607XX) was received from JSR (Tokyo, Japan).

The O-GQDs were synthesized according to a previously reported method [48]; the synthesis route is outlined in Fig. 1(a). Initially, ethanol (25 mL) was poured into a round-bottom flask, and citric acid (1.0 g) was added and dissolved by stirring. A solution of octadecylamine (0.75 g) in ethanol (20 mL) was then added to the above solution, followed by stirring at 25 °C for 1 h. Next, the resulting precipitate was isolated by filtration and washed with ethanol three times. After drying in a convection oven at 65 °C for 24 h, the white solid product was mixed with glycine (0.3 g) in a 20 mL glass vial. This mixture was then calcinated at 200 °C for 3 h to obtain the final product.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Preparation of homeotropically oriented LC cell with O-GQDs
ce:section.ce:para: The O-GQDs were stirred at various concentrations (0.05, 0.1, 0.5, and 1.0 wt%) in the presence of the nematic LCs for 20 min at the nematic-to-isotropic phase transition temperature (T NI = 75 °C), after which the LC mixture was homogenized through ultrasonication. To analyze the electro-optical performance of each nanostructured O-GQD layer, an LC test cell was prepared using bare ITO glass. After washing two pieces of ITO glass, the UV-curable sealant was applied to two parallel edges, and the two pieces of glass were assembled into a cell. The gap between the two ITO glass pieces was maintained using the spherical spacer, and the UV sealant was cured through UV exposure with an energy of 3 J cm−2. To construct the nanostructured O-GQD layer for LC alignment, the mixture of O-GQD and LCs was injected into the LC test cell by capillary force. After sealing two open edges and annealing at 100 °C for 1 h, the test cell was slowly cooled to 25 °C, resulting in the spontaneous self-construction of an O-GQD layer on the ITO substrates.

Fourier transform infrared (FT-IR) spectra were obtained using a Thermo Scientific Nicolet iS20 FTIR spectrometer. The PL spectra were recorded using an Agilent Cary Eclipse fluorescence spectrophotometer. To confirm the formation of the nanostructured O-GQD layer on the ITO substrate, water contact angle measurements and atomic force microscopy (AFM) were performed using a Phoenix MT-A contact angle analyzer and a PSIA XE-100 system, respectively. The prepared LC test cell was decapsulated and then washed with hexane to remove the remaining LCs. Polarized optical microscopy (POM; Olympus BX51 and Leitz Laborlux 12 Pol S) was carried out to investigate the homeotropic orientation of the LC molecules induced by the O-GQD layer. The electro-optical performance of the LC device was evaluated using an optical table consisting of a 632 nm HeNe laser (JDSU 1135P), a function generator (Agilent 33210A), an oscilloscope (Tektronix TBS1062), and a photodetector (EOT ET-2000).

Fig. 1(b) schematically illustrates the spontaneous interfacial self-assembly of the O-GQDs and the subsequent vertical orientation of the LC molecules inside the LC device. Specifically, the O-GQDs, which were uniformly mixed with the LC molecules, self-assembled at the interface with the ITO electrode in the LC device through polar intermolecular interactions, resulting in the formation of a nanostructured molecular layer of O-GQDs. The long alkyl chains on the nanostructured O-GQD thin layer induced the LC molecules to spontaneously orient vertically through van der Waals interactions [49]. Overall, a vertically oriented LC device was simply manufactured within the cell using O-GQDs synthesized through a one-pot process.

To confirm the chemical structure of the synthesized O-GQDs, FT-IR analysis was performed, as shown in Fig. 2(a). The synthesized O-GQDs revealed a broad OH stretching vibration peak at 3400 cm−1, while a strong peak associated with CO stretching vibrations from the carboxylic acid moieties appeared at 1712 cm−1, and a peak indicating the symmetric stretching vibration of COO− groups was observed at 1412 cm−1. In addition, a CH stretching peak attributed to the long alkyl chain of the octadecylamine moiety appeared at 2900 cm−1, and the CC skeletal vibration originating from the aromatic rings of the O-GQDs resulted in a peak at 1530 cm−1 [50,51]. The above results confirmed that the long alkyl chains of octadecylamine were successfully incorporated into the O-GQDs.

To confirm the luminescence characteristics of the O-GQDs, they were uniformly dispersed in hexane and observed under visible and UV light. As shown in Fig. 2(b), the O-GQD solution was almost colorless under ambient light, but it emitted blue light upon UV irradiation. In addition, Fig. 2(c) shows the PL spectra of the O-GQDs over a range of excitation wavelengths. Overall, the O-GQDs showed emission peaks between 400 and 500 nm. As the excitation wavelength increased, the emission spectrum shifted toward longer wavelengths [52]. In particular, the strongest emission peak appeared at 460 nm at an excitation wavelength of 380 nm.

To effectively align the LC molecules in the device, the O-GQDs must be anchored onto the ITO substrate, thereby forming a thin, nanostructured layer of O-GQDs with alkyl side chains. Thus, an LC cell containing the O-GQDs was fabricated and then separated to analyze the O-GQD structure at the interface. Subsequently, water contact angle analysis was performed on the coated ITO surface to verify the formation of the nanostructured O-GQD layer. In general, GQDs exhibit high hydrophilicity, with contact angles of ≤40° owing to the abundance of oxygenated functional groups in their structure [53]. However, because the synthesized O-GQDs each contain several long alkyl chains from the octadecylamine units, the O-GQD layer was more hydrophobic than the bare ITO layer, as shown in Fig. 3(a). In addition, as the concentration of the O-GQDs increased from 0.05 to 1.0 wt%, the contact angle on the layer increased from 81.3° to 102.4°. This hydrophobicity suggests that the hydrophilic groups of the O-GQDs, such as carboxyl or hydroxyl groups, are arranged in the direction of the ITO substrate and that their hydrophobic alkyl chains are organized toward the air interface. In particular, the contact angle of the 0.1 wt% O-GQD solution (i.e., 89.2°) was slightly higher than that of a conventional PI layer (89°) [54]. Therefore, the spontaneous formation of the nanostructured O-GQD layer on the ITO substrate resulted in a hydrophobic surface similar to that observed in conventional PI for controlling the LC orientation.

The nanoscale surface morphology of the self-constructed O-GQD layer was investigated through AFM. Fig. 3(b) shows a three-dimensional (3D) AFM image of the nanostructured O-GQD layer formed with the 0.1 wt% O-GQD dispersion. This image demonstrates that spontaneously self-assembled O-GQDs generate a rough surface morphology with dense nanopillars on the ITO substrate; however, the root mean square roughness of the self-constructed O-GQD layer is only 1.43 nm, suggesting this nanostructured layer is ultrathin.

In order to examine the PL characteristics of the LC mixtures doped with O-GQDs at various concentrations, these mixtures were observed under visible and UV light, as shown in Fig. 4(a). As the O-GQD concentration increased, the solution became darker under ambient light, and a heterogeneous LC mixture appeared. Under UV light, the LC mixtures containing up to 0.1 wt% O-GQDs exhibited remarkable blue light emission, while at higher concentrations of ≥0.5 wt%, this emission disappeared owing to the severe aggregation of the phase-separated O-GQDs. Indeed, in the PL spectra of the actual LC-O-GQD solution (Fig. 4(b)), the emission peak was barely observed at these higher concentrations. However, at the lower O-GQD concentrations, strong blue emission peaks appeared at 450 nm, which were similar to that of pure O-GQDs, indicating that the unique PL emission of pristine O-GQDs in the blue region was maintained even in an LC mixture doped with a low concentration of O-GQDs. Notably, the LC mixture exhibited a relatively weak PL emission at the same concentration of 0.1 wt% compared to that of pure O-GQDs in hexane owing to the UV absorption of LC molecules.

To analyze the LC alignment properties of the nanostructured O-GQD layer, test LC devices were fabricated with different O-GQD concentrations, and Fig. 5 (a–e) show the POM images of these devices with a conventional PI layer and the nanostructured O-GQD layers. When pure LC molecules are not vertically aligned, they usually exhibit an optical texture with a declination line in the cross-polarized state. On the other hand, if LC molecules arrange vertically, a dark image is observed in the cross-polarized state. As shown in Fig. 5(a), the LC device prepared using the conventional PI layer not only presented a clean black image but also demonstrated a conoscopic image of a Maltese cross, thus confirming the vertical arrangement of LC molecules [55]. Similarly, Fig. 5(b) and (c) show that the LC devices prepared using the nanostructured O-GQD layer maintained the black state and Maltese cross image up to a concentration of 0.1 wt%. However, at 0.5 and 1.0 wt% (Fig. 5(d) and (e), respectively), light leaked, resulting in a disclination line in the cross-polarized state. Moreover, the Maltese cross image is blurred, and defects appear in the parallel-polarized state. These results are attributed to the heterogeneous phase-separated aggregation of O-GQDs in the LC device at high O-GQD concentrations, which results in the non-uniform vertical arrangement of LC molecules. At lower concentrations of <0.05 wt%, the poor coverage of O-GQDs on the ITO surface induced light leakage in some areas, thereby leading to the irregular vertical orientation of LCs in the entire device.

To assess the electro-optical performance of the LC device containing the nanostructured O-GQD layer, the electro-optical characteristics were measured, including the voltage–transmittance (V–T) curve and response time, as shown in Fig. 5(f) and (g), respectively. In the V–T curve, the vertical anchoring force of the LC molecules is compared through a threshold voltage, which is defined as the voltage at a transmittance of 10%. Evidently, the threshold voltage of the nanostructured O-GQD layer increases to 2.41 V at a concentration of 0.1 wt%, whereas in the presence of higher O-GQD concentrations (≥0.5 wt%), the threshold voltage decreased to 2.28 V at a concentration of 1.0 wt% owing to the unstable vertical arrangement of LCs stemming from O-GQD aggregation. Therefore, when the nanostructured O-GQD layer was using 0.1 wt% O-GQDs, the homeotropic alignment performance for LCs was higher than that of the conventional PI layer.

Furthermore, the LC switching speed of the devices, an important performance marker for actual LC displays, was investigated. Owing to the particularly thin nanostructured layer formed by the O-GQDs, these devices exhibited a faster falling response time than for the device based on the thick conventional PI layer. In particular, the LC device prepared using the 0.1 wt% O-GQDs, which demonstrated the most stable vertical arrangement characteristics, showed the highest response speed of 4 ms, which is a 375% improvement over that of the conventional PI (i.e., 15 ms). Moreover, the LC devices prepared using an O-GQD concentration of >0.1 wt% showed lower response speeds, which is consistent with the POM observations suggesting aggregation and the inferior V–T curves. It was therefore concluded that the use of the nanostructured O-GQD layer to fabricate LC devices more effectively controlled the LC molecular orientation than the use of a conventional PI layer, thereby delivering superior electro-optical performance.

A novel approach for preparing a nanostructured orientation layer was proposed using surface-functionalized GQDs containing long alkyl chains. Using only a small amount of O-GQDs prepared through a facile one-pot synthesis, a nanostructured O-GQD layer spontaneously self-assembled in an LC cell, wherein it induced the uniform homeotropic orientation of the LC molecules at the interface between the LC and ITO substrate. In addition, the strong blue PL emission of pure O-GQDs was maintained, even in the LC mixture with a low O-GQD concentration. The proposed approach based on the ultrathin nanostructured O-GQD layer facilitates electro-optical performance superior to that of conventional LC devices with a thick polymer layer. In particular, the nanostructured O-GQD layer exhibited an increased threshold voltage of 2.41 V, an improvement over that of a conventional PI layer, and an ultrafast switching speed of 4 ms, representing a 375% improvement over that of the PI-based device. This proposed approach is cost-effective and facile, thereby rendering it suitable for large-area applications, because it does not require additional layer formation processes. We thus believe that our nanostructured O-GQD layers will have potential for application in flexible and functional displays. Further study for the development of flexible, color-switchable LC displays based on the nanostructured O-GQD layer is in progress in this lab.

ce:conflict-of-interest.ce:para: The authors declare that they have no known competing financial interests or personal relationships that could have appeared to influence the work reported in this paper.

ce:acknowledgment.ce:para: This work was supported by a National Research Foundation of Korea (NRF) grant funded by the Korea Government (MSIT) (No. NRF-2018R1A5A1024127).
